data.title: 5 Chinese nationals killed, 8 others injured in Bali car crash
data.description:
Five Chinese nationals were killed and eight others injured in a car crash in Bali, Indonesia, according to China’s consulate on the island on Friday.
The accident happened in northern Bali’s Buleleng regency, the consulate said in a statement.
The Indonesian driver sustained minor injuries, it added.
The 13 Chinese citizens were travelling in a minibus to Bali’s northern side when the driver lost control of the vehicle, veering off the road into a garden and striking a tree, according to Ida Bagus Widwan Sutadi, Buleleng regency’s police chief.
“Due to the driver’s lack of caution when turning and descending, he veered off the asphalt road and into a community garden, causing a traffic accident,” the police chief said in a statement.
The driver was arrested, he added.

Images from the accident site showed that the front end of the minibus had been mangled by the impact, its windscreen blown out and the side doors bent out of shape.
The Chinese consulate said it was “maintaining close communication with Indonesian police and other relevant authorities to verify the casualties involving Chinese citizens”.
The diplomatic mission added that it was “establishing direct contact with those affected and coordinating local consular-protection volunteer groups to provide translation services for them”.
Bali is a popular resort destination. In 2024, Indonesia received nearly 14 million visitors from around the world, according to official data.
Of those, nearly 1.2 million were from China, making China one of Indonesia’s top sources of tourists, behind only Malaysia, Australia and Singapore.
Road accidents are common in Indonesia as safety standards and infrastructure can often be poor.
Chinese tourists have been involved in a number of tragic road accidents while travelling overseas.
In May, five Chinese tourists were killed in a car crash near the United States’ Yellowstone National Park as their tour van collided with a pickup truck. An Italian tourist and the American pickup truck driver also died in the crash.
In one of the most fatal cases, 32 Chinese tourists were killed in a bus crash in North Korea in 2018.

data.title: China signals patience in Nexperia row – but escalation seen as possible as talks loom
data.description:
As China and the Netherlands prepare to hold talks next week over the Nexperia chipmaker dispute, Beijing has the patience to resolve the issue through multiple rounds of negotiations to ensure the case remains isolated, analysts said.
On Thursday, Dutch Economy Minister Vincent Karremans said a senior government delegation would visit Beijing “to continue our efforts to find a mutually agreeable solution”.
The Dutch government took the unusual step of seizing control of Nexperia from its Chinese owners on September 30, citing national security concerns and invoking a law that had been dormant since 1952. Dutch court filings suggest this was done under US pressure, with Washington announcing its expanded trade blacklist the day before.
Beijing responded by imposing export controls on certain components made by Nexperia’s China unit, threatening global carmaker supply chains.
After the latest round of US-China trade talks, Beijing agreed to exempt compliant orders for chips produced at Nexperia’s China facilities from export bans. Washington also suspended its new entity list rules for a year.
However, the Dutch chipmaker’s European fabrication facilities have yet to resume wafer supplies to Nexperia’s China unit since last month’s suspension.
Cui Hongjian, director of the European Studies Centre at Beijing Foreign Studies University, said the resumption of these supplies would be a crucial and positive step.


“If the Dutch side fails to make any positive statements this time or remains stagnant in its position, I believe the likelihood of this issue being prolonged – or the possibility that China may take countermeasures – could increase,” he added.
Dutch minister Karremans welcomed Beijing’s decision to relax export rules for the chips, though he added that the Netherlands and other countries would continue to monitor the situation to “ascertain if and when trade from Nexperia’s facilities in China is fully resumed”.
“Predictable security of supply of these essential chips and mutual dependence are crucial for our industry and supply chains globally. The Netherlands remains committed to diplomatic engagement with China,” he added.
On Thursday, Ministry of Commerce spokesman He Yadong said Beijing had held several rounds of talks with the Dutch side, maintaining “a responsible attitude for the stability and security of the global semiconductor industry and supply chains”.
“We hope the Dutch side will demonstrate the sincerity to cooperate with China, come up with substantial and constructive solutions to the issue as soon as possible, and take real action to promptly and effectively restore the security and stability of the global semiconductor industry and supply chains.”
Cui of Beijing Foreign Studies University noted that while Beijing wants to resolve the dispute, it recognises that handling the issue on a case-by-case basis could give the impression that similar actions might be tolerated in the future.
He added that Beijing had shifted over the past year or two from avoiding trade conflicts to becoming less concerned about the potential for escalation.




Beijing has also sought to increase pressure on the Netherlands by raising the issue with other European countries in recent weeks.
At a meeting on Tuesday with the Spanish Minister of Economy, Trade and Business Carlos Cuerpo, Commerce Minister Wang Wentao brought up the Nexperia case, saying he hopes “the Dutch side will act responsibly to translate its verbal statements into concrete actions, revoke the relevant measures as soon as possible [and] correct its wrong practices,” according to an official statement.
Cuerpo said Spain was willing to communicate with the Dutch side, as a stable and smooth global semiconductor supply chain benefits all parties, the Chinese statement added.
So far, the dispute has remained a bilateral issue centred on the semiconductor supply chain between China and the Netherlands and has not escalated to the European Union level, Cui said.
According to Wang Yiwei, director of the Centre for European Studies at Renmin University of China, Beijing recognises that the Netherlands’ actions resulted from US pressure – though Dutch officials deny any American involvement.
“So as long as we return to the spirit of contracts, market principles and free trade, these issues can be resolved. Given the current easing of tensions between China and the US, I think it is even more appropriate to engage in calm and measured negotiations with the Dutch government,” Wang said.
Jens Eskelund, president of the European Union Chamber of Commerce in China, said the fallout over Nexperia has the potential to seriously impact both European and Chinese businesses.
“The Chamber will therefore be monitoring developments closely and hopes to see a negotiated solution announced as soon as possible,” he said.

data.title: ONE Championship: Thai star Stamp Fairtex to make up for lost time against Kana Morimoto
data.description:
Stamp Fairtex has all the elements of a star. She was the first three-sport world champion in ONE Championship history. Her walkouts are iconic, her smile is infectious and her personality has made her one of the most beloved figures in Asian combat sports.
But it has not all been Korean hearts and TikTok dances for the crowd favourite.
The Thai superstar has endured one of the toughest spells of her career, forced to relinquish her atomweight Muay Thai belt last year because of a chronic knee injury that kept her sidelined for months.
Now, at ONE 173 in Tokyo and complete with new-look bleached blonde hair, she makes her long-awaited return against Japan’s Kana Morimoto in a kickboxing bout that will test both her timing and her patience.
“I miss everything,” said Stamp, who has been atomweight world champion in MMA, Muay Thai and kickboxing. “I miss the fans, I miss the cheering and I even miss that sound when we fight – it’s something that always motivates me.”

The 27-year-old admitted that the time away was more difficult than any fight camp. Unable to train or compete, she had to rely on the support of her family and teammates at Fairtex Gym in Pattaya.
“It was really hard. I couldn’t train at all – and I train every day, so that was a bad time for me,” she said. “But my family supported me and kept telling me I could come back.”
Her return comes not in her preferred disciplines of Muay Thai or MMA, but in kickboxing – a rule set she has not competed under since 2021.
“Honestly, I don’t miss kickboxing,” she said with a laugh. “I miss Muay Thai and MMA more, but this is a warm-up fight for me. I just want to test myself and feel how my body reacts again.”


Her opponent, Morimoto, is a former K-1 champion and brings both skill and experience to the ring. Stamp knows it will be no easy comeback.
“She’s really good. She’s fought some of the best, and she has a lot of experience. It’s a challenge for sure, but that’s what I want. If I fight someone good, that’s good for me too.”
Despite the long lay-off, Stamp’s trademark confidence remains intact. When asked what she expects from the fight, she smiled and replied, “I expect she loses.”
The Bangkok native has also been turning heads with her improved English, something she credits to daily practice and support from her fiancé, Anthony Duong, who is also involved in MMA.












View this post on Instagram























A post shared by Anthony Duong (@duonganthony_)



Back in Japan, a country with a deep martial arts history, Stamp feels the weight of expectation but also the excitement that comes with performing on one of the biggest cards of the year.
“It’s Japan. You have to look good and perform well. Even my Dad said my hair looks good,” she joked, showing off her new blonde streaks.
After the fight, she plans to unwind with some well-deserved Tokyo desserts. “Maybe shabu-shabu or hotpot, and pancakes – the really fluffy ones,” she said. “I miss that.”
As for the rest of the card, Stamp says she was especially excited for the Superbon v Masaaki Noiri and Rukiya Anpo v Marat Grigorian matchups but remained confident the women would steal the show.
“Everyone is talking about Rodtang, Noiri, and Superbon, but I think this women’s fight will be Fight of the Night.”

data.title: Modi’s coalition set for big win in key Indian state election, bolstering his popularity
data.description:
Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi’s ruling coalition is headed to win a key state election in a vote seen as a crucial test of Modi’s popularity in one of the country’s poorest yet most politically influential states.
Partial results on Friday from the Election Commission of India, the country’s election watchdog, showed the Bharatiya Janata Party-led National Democratic Alliance (NDA) expected to take 208 seats of the 243-member legislature in the eastern state of Bihar.
A simple majority to form the government is 122. Modi’s party alone is forecast to take 95 seats.
A victory in Bihar, the country’s third-most populous state with nearly 130 million people, is crucial as it sends the fourth-highest number of lawmakers to the lower house of parliament.
Its control strengthens the party in power as the state is seen as a political bellwether, setting political trends across India’s Hindi-speaking heartland.
This year, the two-phase election was held amid widespread public concerns over unemployment, law and order, and alleged irregularities in revision of electoral rolls.
It saw hard-fought campaigns marked by mass rallies, aggressive welfare messaging and personal outreach to voters, both by Modi and his opponents.
As the NDA led in the initial counting, celebrations erupted in the state capital, Patna, with supporters waving party flags, dancing to the beats of drums and setting off firecrackers.

The election in the agrarian state was a crucial test for Modi as he sought to build momentum ahead of national elections in 2029 and key state polls in Uttar Pradesh, West Bengal and Assam over the next two years.
The victory would give a timely lift to the federal government, which continues to govern without a full parliamentary majority since last year’s national election forced the ruling party to rely on regional allies.
Modi’s party forged an alliance with Janata Dal (United) and Lok Janshakti Party (Ramvilas) to form the federal government. These alliances are Modi’s key partners in Bihar too.
“It will give great comfort to Modi to get Bihar under his belt. It will provide more stability to the government at the centre,” said Neerja Chowdhury, a political analyst.
The BJP leads Bihar’s ruling alliance, with Chief Minister Nitish Kumar of the Janata Dal (United) at the helm. Kumar, 74, has governed for nearly two decades, and is credited with improving the state’s infrastructure and ending its law-and-order problems.
Once an opponent of Modi, Kumar later rejoined the BJP-led NDA.
There were apprehensions that a loss in Bihar could possibly split Kumar’s party and threaten Modi’s federal coalition, which relies on 12 of his lawmakers.

data.title: This woman shares diabetes journey to raise awareness, inspire others to live a full life
data.description:
Former Hongkonger Michelle Hale was 14 when she was diagnosed with type 1 diabetes, a chronic disease in which the body does not produce enough insulin.
Untreated, it causes high blood sugar levels, which can cause organ damage and increase the risks of seizures, heart disease and strokes. Treatment requires a daily regimen of insulin.
Type 1 diabetes is not fully understood, but it is an autoimmune disease that seems to start with a genetic predisposition. An environmental trigger, possibly a virus, causes the immune system to attack and destroy the body’s insulin-producing cells in the pancreas.
Since the pancreas no longer produces insulin, type 1 diabetics must take insulin daily through injections or a pump.
No one in Hale’s family had type 1 diabetes, though. Her diagnosis was all the more surprising given that she has an identical twin who is not diabetic.
Excessive thirst, frequent urination, loss of appetite, loss of weight, blurred vision and vomiting were the symptoms that landed her in the doctor’s office. Her blood sugar levels were dangerously high, and she was sent to the hospital for emergency treatment.

While dealing with the usual teenage angst growing up in Adelaide in South Australia, Hale was injecting insulin daily, monitoring her blood sugar and watching what she was eating.
“I was too old for kids’ diabetes camps and too young for adult diabetes programmes, which left me with no one,” she says. “I felt very alone, with no support, and I felt that way for 17 years.”
Types of diabetes
Type 1 cases are far rarer than type 2 diabetes cases, which have skyrocketed in number. Type 2 is often the result of lifestyle factors such as poor diet and lack of exercise. It accounts for about 90 per cent of all diabetes cases worldwide. Genetic factors can increase the risk of type 2 diabetes.
In type 2 diabetes, the body either does not use insulin well, a condition called insulin resistance, or the pancreas cannot make enough insulin to keep up with the demand. Treatment often begins with lifestyle changes in diet and exercise, as well as glucose-lowering medications. Insulin may be needed later as the disease progresses and the pancreas tires out.
Another much rarer form of diabetes, known as Mody – for maturity-onset diabetes of the young – is a genetic type that was sometimes loosely referred to as type 3 diabetes. This unofficial term has since become more commonly applied to Alzheimer’s disease as researchers explore the links between Alzheimer’s and insulin resistance that occurs specifically in the brain.

Gestational diabetes – gestational diabetes mellitus or GDM – develops during pregnancy, when hormones from the placenta interfere with the mother’s insulin uptake, leading to insulin resistance. While GDM usually goes away after childbirth, it increases health risks for mother and baby. Having had GDM puts a mother at a higher risk of developing type 2 diabetes later in life.
Prediabetes is a precursor to diabetes in which blood sugar levels are elevated but not yet in the diabetic range.
Worldwide diabetes numbers and the health risks
Globally, around 537 million adults – about 10.5 per cent – are living with diabetes. By 2045, this is projected to rise to 783 million or 12.2 per cent.
While the world’s population is estimated to grow 20 per cent over this period, the number with diabetes is estimated to increase by 46 per cent.
Aside from the physical risks, diabetes has been shown to affect mental health, with detrimental effects on self-image and self-esteem, leading to emotional upset and social anxiety.
It can also trigger eating disorders, including diabulimia – when someone with type 1 diabetes restricts or completely omits their insulin doses to lose weight or prevent weight gain. This puts the person at risk of incredibly dangerous conditions, such as diabetic ketoacidosis.

The emotional weight of living with diabetes can lead to diabetes burnout, depression and even thoughts of death, Hale says.
Sharing her experience to help others
It was only when Hale was pregnant with her firstborn son, TJ, now aged eight, and was about to move from Dubai to Hong Kong with her husband, Trevor, that she chose to switch the narrative of her diabetes from negative to positive.
Until then, she had at best ignored it as far as possible, and at worst felt shame and tried to hide it. She decided to celebrate her diabetes to help inspire others.
Hale launched a website, Global Diabetic, and the @globaldiabetic social media platform, both dedicated to empowering individuals living with type 1 diabetes.
Starting with a pregnancy shoot in which she wore a continuous glucose monitor, she was determined to show that living with diabetes should not present barriers to living a full life.
She went on to create content about the experiences she was having as a diabetic, from skydiving to climbing Mount Kilimanjaro, as well as showing off her insulin pump and monitor while wearing glamorous dresses at red carpet events or poolside in a bikini.

“I started Global Diabetic to show that people living with diabetes can live a fabulous life. Women reach out to me all the time, thanking me for showing them it’s OK to be loud and proud about wearing medical devices, removing shame and stigma,” she says.
“I’ve shown my pregnancy pictures wearing my diabetes gadgets, and women have told me that it inspired them to have babies. Seeing someone like me, just a woman with type 1 diabetes and now a mum sharing her story, gives real hope,” she adds.
In a lighthearted way, she also shares how she manages her diabetes, including how to use tools, such as pumps, sensors and apps that help count carbohydrates and other nutrients and track exercise levels; what she takes while travelling in case her luggage or carry-on bags are lost; and how she plans meals.
Celebrities speak out on managing diabetes
Diabetes advocacy has risen in recent years, with celebrities such as Tom Hanks, Halle Berry and Nick Jonas using their platforms to promote awareness in the community.
Hanks first opened up about his type 2 diabetes diagnosis in 2013, blaming a mixture of genes and his unhealthy eating patterns; recently, he has had to warn followers of fake advertisements using his likeness to promote diabetes “cures”.
Berry was diagnosed with diabetes at the age of 22, which was said to be type 1. Later, she announced that she instead had type 2 diabetes. She has managed her condition in part through adopting the keto diet.
Jonas was diagnosed with type 1 diabetes at the age of 13 and learned to manage it with resilience. His message for others: “Know that you can still do anything you want to do with your life. I didn’t let it slow me down, and you don’t have to either.”
Retreats cater for diabetic clients

A growing number of five-star hotels now offer retreats for clients with type 2 diabetes who want to learn more about how to manage their condition in a luxury space.
The Amanpuri in Phuket, Thailand, is an example. It combines medical and wellness expertise to cater to diabetic guests’ needs, to help them incorporate dietary changes, exercise and other wellness activities to manage their condition.
Another is Ananda in the Himalayas, overlooking India’s Ganges River. It offers a two-week programme that incorporates Ayurveda, yoga, traditional Oriental therapies, physiotherapy and nutrition, and is designed to keep blood sugar levels and weight in check and to boost energy and overall well-being.
Its head, Dr Sreelal Sankar, says the resort’s commitment to “holistic diabetes care” has had good results, including improvements in clients’ blood glucose, insulin resistance and weight. Some guests have been able to lower their dependence on medication after the retreat, he adds.

Mayrlife in Austria is another. It runs a two-week detox programme for those seeking a natural and holistic approach to improve diabetes symptoms. It aims to help guests better understand how healthy eating, regular exercise and lifestyle changes can help to keep diabetes under better control.
For Hale, the growth in awareness is a positive for the diabetic community.
“The loneliness that diabetes creates can be healed beyond Western medicine, and these luxury retreats and hotels are offering what we need – a glow up to diabetes.”
Like what you read? Follow SCMP Lifestyle on Facebook, X and Instagram. You can also sign up for our eNewsletter here.

data.title: India’s No 1 player gets Chinese visa for grand slam tennis qualifier after appeal
data.description:
India’s top-ranked male tennis player has received a Chinese visa for an Australian Open wild-card playoff tournament beginning on November 24 in Chengdu after first being denied one.
Sumit Nagal took to social media on Tuesday to ask the Chinese ambassador to India, Xu Feihong, and the Chinese embassy to intervene on his behalf.
In a second social media post late on Thursday, Nagal thanked the Chinese ambassador and India’s ministry of external affairs, among others, after receiving the visa.
“A big thank you … for their prompt help in helping me secure the visa in time,” Nagal wrote.
In his original post, Nagal said “my visa was rejected without reason. Your urgent help would be much appreciated.”

A big thank you to @Media_SAI, @MEAIndia, @China_Amb_India and @ChinaSpox_India for their prompt help in helping me secure the visa in time 🙏 https://t.co/RRawWvxrzA
— Sumit Nagal (@nagalsumit) November 13, 2025

Nagal gave no indication in his second post as to why his original visa request was denied.
One of the 28-year-old Nagal’s career highlights was taking the opening set of a US Open match against Roger Federer in 2019. But Federer won the next three sets to advance to the second round.
Nagal had a career-high singles ranking of 68 in July 2024, but his current ranking is 290. He has been a member of India’s Davis Cup team since 2018.
In January, he lost in the first round at the Australian Open and failed to make it through qualifying for the French Open and Wimbledon.

He won the 2015 Wimbledon boys’ doubles title with Vietnam’s Ly Hoang Nam, becoming the sixth Indian player to win a junior grand slam title.
The Australian Open’s Asia-Pacific wild-card playoff – a 16-player men’s and women’s singles event and 12 teams in men’s and women’s doubles – is scheduled for November 24-29 at the Sichuan International Tennis Centre.
The 2026 Australian Open, the first grand slam event of the year, begins on January 18 in Melbourne.

data.title: Beijing criticises first Taiwan arms deal since Donald Trump’s return to White House
data.description:
Beijing has strongly criticised the latest possible US arms sale to Taiwan, accusing Washington of grossly violating the one-China principle.
On Thursday, the Pentagon announced that the US had approved a deal to sell US$330 million worth of fighter jet parts to the island.
It is the first arms deal with Taipei since President Donald Trump returned to the White House in January.
Beijing’s foreign ministry said the deal seriously violated the one-China principle and “gravely infringed upon China’s sovereignty and security interests”.
“China will take all necessary measures to resolutely defend national sovereignty, security and territorial integrity,” ministry spokesman Lin Jian said on Friday.
He also urged Washington to honour the commitments made by US leaders and stop supporting what it described as “Taiwan independence” forces.
Beijing sees Taiwan as part of China to be reunited by force if necessary. The United States, in common with most countries, does not recognise Taiwan as an independent state, but is opposed to any attempt to take the self-ruled island by force and is committed to supplying it with weapons to defend itself.
The Pentagon said the deal would improve Taiwan’s capability to “meet current and future threats” by maintaining operational readiness.
The package includes non-standard components, spare and repair parts, consumables and accessories, as well as repair and return support for F-16 fighters, C-130 Hercules transport planes and Indigenous Defence Fighter aircraft, according to the Pentagon.
The government in Taipei thanked Washington for continuing the policy of regular arms sales to the island and for its support in boosting its self-defence capabilities and resilience.
“The deepening of the Taiwan-US security partnership is an important cornerstone of peace and stability in the Indo-Pacific region,” Karen Kuo, a spokeswoman for the island’s leader, said on Friday.
The Pentagon’s announcement of the deal came weeks after Trump’s meeting with his Chinese counterpart, Xi Jinping, in Busan, South Korea.


Despite the many issues discussed, Trump told reporters that Taiwan “never came up” during the meeting.
But the issue was raised during the first in-person meeting between US Defence Secretary Pete Hegseth and his Chinese counterpart Dong Jun, a day after the Trump-Xi summit.
Dong said the US should be cautious in its words and actions on the Taiwan issue and must unequivocally oppose Taiwanese independence. Meanwhile, Hegseth underlined Washington’s concerns about the Taiwan Strait.
The two sides agreed that peace, stability and good relations were “the best path” for the two countries, according to the US defence secretary.
Earlier this month, Hegseth said Beijing and Washington had “agreed that we should set up military-to-military channels to deconflict and de-escalate any problems that arise”.

data.title: Hong Kong’s fourth local chikungunya case ‘may be linked to new infection source’
data.description:
Hong Kong’s fourth locally reported case of chikungunya fever could have resulted from a new source of infection unrelated to previous ones, a leading medical expert has warned, adding that heightened surveillance by authorities could uncover more patients.
Professor Jasper Chan Fuk-woo of the University of Hong Kong’s microbiology department issued the warning on Friday, a day after a 68-year-old woman living in Shau Kei Wan was listed as the city’s fourth locally acquired case.
“Based on the epidemiological investigation findings released so far, this new case likely represents a new source of infection not linked to the previous three local cases,” he told the Post.
“Genomic analysis of the virus strain should provide further information on this,” he said, adding that such a discovery raised concerns of undetected ongoing transmission chains.
The latest patient developed joint pain on Monday and sought help at Pamela Youde Nethersole Eastern Hospital in Chai Wan after suffering from a fever and rash the next day.
Health authorities said she had no travel history for the past two months, adding that her residence and usual activity areas did not overlap with neighbourhoods where existing cases had been found in recent months.
Genome analysis was being conducted by the government to confirm whether there were epidemiological linkages, they said.
The previous three cases lived in Wong Tai Sin and Kwai Tsing, with one of them working in Sheung Wan.
“There may be more local cases being detected, especially with the proactive surveillance by the Centre for Health Protection and also the public being better aware of the situation through the media,” he said.
“At this stage, it is not considered endemic.”
He urged anyone with matching symptoms, irrespective of their travel or residence history, to seek medical advice and testing for early diagnosis and management, as cases could rise in December during major sports, music or entertainment events.


Chan earlier warned that the city could face a higher risk of chikungunya fever spreading during the National Games, which are currently being held in Hong Kong, Macau and Guangdong province.
Hong Kong on Thursday also reported an imported case involving an 83-year-old man living in Foshan, Guangdong province.
He visited Hong Kong on November 10 and was staying in Sham Shui Po when he sought help at Princess Margaret Hospital for a fever and joint pain.
The number of confirmed cases in Hong Kong this year stood at 61 as of 5pm on Thursday. Aside from the four local cases, the rest are imported.
According to government data, the Area Gravidtrap Index, which monitors the distribution and breeding activity of the Aedes albopictus mosquito, for Shau Kei Wan and Sai Wan Ho fell from its peak of 31.5 per cent in May to 6.5 per cent in October.
The Aedes albopictus mosquito is a common vector for chikungunya and dengue fever.
The department stipulates that an index reading of between 5 and 19 per cent falls into the second mildest of the four-tier system for the distribution of mosquitoes in the survey area.
The four tiers are “not extensive”, “fairly extensive”, “extensive” and “very extensive”.
Eastern district councillor Wong Chi-chung said index levels shared at the council meeting over the past month indicated that the area’s mosquito situation was under control, adding that levels had been low for the past month or so.
“We’ve been reminding residents to clear or report stagnant water. We’ve been on high alert,” he said, adding that his staff had been making hundreds of individual phone calls to residents to raise awareness.
The Food and Environmental Hygiene Department said it had immediately enhanced its mosquito control on Thursday evening in the vicinity of the Tung Hei Court housing estate, where the latest locally infected patient lives.
“[The department has carried out] intensive fogging in scrubby areas within a 250-metre radius of the relevant locations to kill adult mosquitoes,” it added.
The department said that the removal of stagnant water and the application of insecticides were also being conducted.

data.title: Singapore Airlines profit drops 68% amid Air India losses and rising costs
data.description:
Singapore Airlines on Thursday reported a sharp fall in first-half profit, hit by losses at its Indian associate Air India, higher costs and intensifying competition.
The airline’s profit slid steeply in the second quarter to S$52 million (US$40.18 million) from S$186 million in the first quarter, highlighting Air India’s drag on performance.
Singapore Airlines began accounting for Air India’s earnings in December 2024 after the integration of joint venture Vistara into Air India. The Singapore carrier has a 25.1 per cent stake in the Indian airline.
Air India is still reeling from a fatal crash earlier this year and is seeking at least 100 billion rupees (US$1.1 billion) in financial support from its owners, Tata Sons and Singapore Air, Bloomberg News reported last month. Singapore Airlines reiterated its commitment to collaborating with Tata Sons on Air India’s turnaround plan.

Passenger demand remained strong and fuel costs fell, even as competition in key markets squeezed yields.
Singapore’s flag carrier sought to keep investors onside with a capital return plan and a confident outlook for travel demand heading into the year-end rush.
It unveiled a three-year special dividend plan totalling about S$900 million, paying 10 Singapore cents per share annually. It also announced an interim dividend of 5 Singapore cents a share.
Half-year net profit fell to S$239 million, down from S$742 million last year and missing the S$341.9 million consensus estimate from Visible Alpha.
The share of results from associated companies plunged S$417 million, largely because of Air India’s losses. Rising non-fuel expenses from inflation and network growth erased much of the benefit from cheaper jet fuel, keeping overall costs on an upwards path.
The drop is not far off our forecast decline of 74 per cent for fiscal 2026, said Lorraine Tan, director at Morningstar. “Much of the decline is due to a normalisation of passenger yields post Covid.”
Revenues rose by 1.9 per cent to a first-half record of S$9.68 billion.
In the first quarter, the company had flagged unpredictable demand for its cargo business due to tariffs.
The cargo business remains uncertain, with yields under pressure as airlines shift capacity away from the United States to other routes despite rising volumes, the airline said.
Additional reporting by Bloomberg

data.title: Hong Kong’s top developers vie for rare Tsuen Wan site in test of land market rebound
data.description:
Nine groups, including some of Hong Kong’s largest developers – Sun Hung Kai Properties (SHKP), CK Asset Holdings, Henderson Land and Chinachem Group – have submitted offers for a 70,127 sq ft parcel in Tsuen Wan in the New Territories.
The Lands Department received the bids by noon on Friday, the deadline for the tender that began on September 26, according to a spokeswoman, who did not provide further details.
The developers confirmed their participation in the tender to the Post.
The site at the junction of Wing Shun Street and Texaco Road in Tsuen Wan is designated for non-industrial purposes and is estimated to provide about 780 average-sized flats, according to Secretary for Development Bernadette Linn Hon-ho in comments made in July.
About 940 square metres (10,118 sq ft) must be set aside for social welfare facilities, including a centre for home care services for elderly people and a residential special childcare centre, among others.
Being the only residential site to be offered by the government in the second quarter of its financial year, the plot “presents a compelling development opportunity”, said Hannah Jeong, executive director and head of valuation and advisory services at CBRE Hong Kong.

Jeong said flats built on the site could fetch between HK$16,000 (US$2,059) and HK$22,000 per square foot. Currently, residential prices in the vicinity range from HK$12,000 to HK$18,000 per square foot.
“Its proximity to the MTR station, well-established infrastructure, well-developed commercial and retail complexes and extensive community amenities – including Tsuen Wan Park and Tsuen Wan Sports Centre – are notable advantages,” she said. “Additionally, the potential for sea views in some units will certainly enhance the site’s appeal.”
The project, however, comes with obligations that are likely to increase development costs, including the construction of two pedestrian bridge connections and a public car park.
The site was also close to industrial building clusters, which could weigh on the developers’ pricing strategy and project positioning, Jeong said.
“Market sentiment remains positive following two interest rate cuts this year, supported by strong primary market sales and favourable recent land transactions,” Jeong said. “Under these conditions, we expect this tender to receive a solid response, potentially at a relatively aggressive price level.”
The Tsuen Wan plot was one of eight residential sites that Financial Secretary Paul Chan Mo-po earmarked for sale in the 2025-26 financial year in his budget speech in February. These plots were expected to provide about 4,500 private flats.
The estimated price per square foot for the total gross floor area of the Tsuen Wan project was expected to range between HK$2,800 and HK$3,000, according to an earlier estimate by CHFT Advisory and Appraisal.
Developers have been more active in landbanking this year amid higher home sales. As of October, 51,361 new and lived-in homes had changed hands, according to official data, putting this year’s residential sales on track to exceed the 53,099 units sold in 2024.
Last week, SHKP’s unit Mega Asia International won a 600,000 sq ft plot in Tuen Mun, which was valued at between HK$1.8 billion and HK$2.3 billion, excluding any profit-sharing arrangements with MTR Corporation, according to CBRE.
MTR Corp put up the Tuen Mun A16 Station Package One property development for tender. The site is expected to provide 1,280 residential units. Five other groups also joined the bidding.
In August, Sino Land acquired a 47,000 sq ft residential site in Tuen Mun known as TMTL 569, which has a gross floor area of 282,000 sq ft and is estimated to provide 525 flats and 251 car parking spaces, for HK$1.09 billion.
Two other residential sites have also been awarded by the Lands Department this year.
In January, SHKP secured a site measuring around 38,750 sq ft in Sha Tin for HK$606 million. The developer also picked up a site of more than 110,000 sq ft in Tung Chung for HK$602 million in February.
